data.title: SEALSQ: Why The Valuation Is Finally Rational
data.description:
SEALSQ Corp (NASDAQ:LAES) is worth a revisit here, following the cautious Hold rating three months ago when I last covered the stock here on Seeking Alpha. The cautious stance seems to have aged well, as the stock is down about 24% since that coverage. In this revisit, I'll look at the company’s progress from both an operational and market perspective, drawing insights from both fundamental and operational metrics. The goal is not to catch the absolute LAES bottom, but to identify an entry where risk and reward align better for investors.

For investors who are not familiar with the name SEALSQ Corp: it is a quantum company that is a subsidiary of the Swiss cybersecurity firm WISeKey International Holding (WKEY). SEALSQ is a pure-play technology firm focused on developing secure semiconductors, Public Key Infrastructure [PKI], and post-quantum technology hardware and software products.

SEALSQ has already developed post-quantum tech that have seen real-world implementation. For example, SEALSQ’s SEALCOIN post-quantum chips have been launched with WiseKey's WISeSat nanosatellite into orbit via SpaceX. As a subsidiary of WiseKey, SEALSQ leverages many of WiseKey's strategic partnerships and assets as an enabler to its growth. Another example of SEALSQ’s technology implementation is the QS7001 secure hardware platform, which is currently being tested to quantum-proof blockchain platforms like Hedera (HBAR-USD). The company boasts over 25 years of expertise in semiconductor secure chip design and has seen its technology implemented in over 1.6 billion devices globally. So the pivot to post-quantum chips comes naturally for the company. There has been a historic inventory glut for traditional chip demand generally and demand has shifted to AI and purpose-built specific chips. Thus, SEALSQ’s pivot to post-quantum chip design is perfectly timed.

Lately, quantum companies have started moving beyond research and into product releases for early-stage use. The sector is now shifting from prototypes and MoUs to practical applications of quantum technology. Sales are shaping up among emerging quantum companies with real products and use cases.

We’ve seen quantum-focused companies like IonQ (IONQ) report $20.7 million in revenue for Q2 2025, which was a ~170% YoY increase, driven by a large commercial deal for the Forte Enterprise system. D-Wave Quantum (QBTS) reported a jump of over 500% to record $15 million in sales in Q1, propelled by a large one-time commercial sale of the Advantage-1 computer system to a client in Germany. Though D-Wave’s Q2 top line was down sequentially due to the Q1 one-time sale, it was still a 40.9% increase YoY. Subsequent launch of D-Wave’s Advantage-2 computing system in Q2 saw QBTS leap higher in stock price. The point I’m stressing is that the market responds strongly to product releases of these quantum stocks, and sales are also shifting from smaller, one-off research-based projects to multi-million dollar, long-term commercial deployment

I believe the stage is set for SEALSQ also, and the Q4 target products release and customer sampling, and then commercialization (target of Q4 2025 to Q1 2026) of its QS7001 platform and the QVault TPM modules are the catalysts in the meantime. SEALSQ successfully completed the cryptographic toolbox validation stage for the QS7001 platform last month and has formally begun the Common Criteria certification process for both the QS7001 platform and the QVault TPM module. Common Criteria EAL5+ certification is expected to be achieved sometime next year.

An analysis of SEALSQ's operations shows a confluence of factors creating a perfect inflection point and timing for a potential breakout in Q4, which would also likely propel the rest of the fiscal year's target sales. If SEALSQ’s FY25 target sales of $16 million to $20 million are met, it would imply a 45% to 82% YoY jump. Such headline top line growth holds the potential to drive a near-term re-rating for the stock.

SEALSQ’s Path to Sustained Sales and Premium Valuation

In the meantime, I think there isn't much to extract from the financials of companies like SEALSQ beyond how the sales pipeline is shaping up and how feasible the sales targets are. The H1 2025 report (released last month) was less about historical performance and more about providing forward-looking guidance and reassuring investors that the growth catalysts are still on track.

Revenue remained flat in H1 2025 because the company's focus has shifted from legacy products (traditional semiconductors and PKI) to next-gen semiconductors and software solutions (post-quantum chips). Near-term liquidity looks very strong, as current assets of $93.1 million exceed current liabilities of $15.5 million. SEALSQ’s cash position also jumped to $120 million in H1, up from $19 million a year ago and $85 million as of 2024 end. The cash increase is a result of $9.15 million from warrant exercises that were converted successfully in early January, then a follow-on equity offering of $20 million in May, that has provided the company with refreshed cash runway.

Growing bookings is an important barometer to assess a company like SEALSQ. The company confirmed in the H1 report that current business pipeline now stands at $145 million of revenue opportunities with both current and prospective clients for the period 2026 to 2028, of which $6.8 million of confirmed bookings was already secured as of Q1 this year.

SEALSQ's bottom line still seems miles away from profitability as the net loss totaled $21.2 million in FY24. Most of these emerging quantum companies are still grappling with high costs, mainly in R&D and sales and marketing. Investing in them is basically a bet on the future, where risk equals reward. Their success hinges greatly on the commercialization of new technology.

SEALSQ's product launch timeline is strategically aligning with the company’s strategic acquisitions. SEALSQ has been actively engaging in acquisitions as a key part of its growth strategy. They have made some full or minority strategic investments in a series of tech companies this year, and the style of acquisition appears to be highly strategic and focused on creating both vertical and technology integration and synergy. The company acquires businesses that complement and enhance their core technological capabilities, especially in the post-quantum and secure semiconductor space.

SEALSQ has completed the acquisition of IC'ALPS, a French specialist in Application-Specific Integrated Circuits [ASIC] design. The acquisition was pending French government approval at the time of the H1 report but has since been finalized. The deal was for a fixed purchase price of €12.5 million, payable in a combination of cash and LAES shares, with a potential earn-out of up to €4 million contingent on IC'ALPS achieving revenue in excess of €11 million for full year 2025. I think that target for IC’ALPs isn't out of reach, as the company reported €9.76 million in revenue for the full year 2024. The consolidation of revenue from IC'ALPS is expected to be a major contributor to SEALSQ’s 2H 2025 revenue surge, thereby helping SEALSQ reach the full-year target $16 million to $20 million.

Looking beyond the immediate impact, the acquisition is not just about immediate revenue increment. It is a strategic move to combine SEALSQ's post-quantum IP with IC'ALPS's ASIC design expertise to create new, high-demand products for the future, which is why the earn-out is tied to IC'ALPS's future performance. This shows that SEALSQ's management has confidence in the long-term synergy of the deal, even if the short-term impact is simply a revenue lift. The IC'ALPS acquisition has the potential to become accretive to the bottom line in the long term because of the combination of revenue and cost synergies that are expected to materialize over time, but it may not be immediately accretive at the moment because IC'ALPS itself is running at a net loss prior to acquisition, reporting net loss of €2.02 million. Investing in LAES would therefore be a long-term play.

The sales and enterprise value valuations of LAES are now more rational compared to late last year and early this year when the quantum hype was in full swing. Several so-called quantum companies with no revenue to show were trading at sales and EV multiples over 1000x at the time. There has been a marked derating since, and quantum stocks without an identifiable revenue model have seen steep corrections.

SEALSQ has moved from the hype-driven bucket of pre-revenue peers, but revenue still seems paltry compared to the company’s lofty promising bookings figures. At current $408 million market cap, $2.70 stock price, and $11 million in trailing twelve month sales, LAES now trades at a more rational valuation of around 37x P/Sales on a trailing twelve-month basis.

As we have established thus far, the revenue targets for FY25 are not unattainable, especially since there will be consolidation of revenue with the acquired companies including IC’ALPS. If the lower end of $16 million revenue is reached, at the current $2.70 stock price LAES will be trading around 26x forward P/S. Compared to peers like IONQ and QBTS with forward P/S at 138x and 220x respectively, LAES is trading on the conservative side.

Risks

With all the positive developments highlighted so far, the quantum sector still remains in its infancy. While SEALSQ exudes enough confidence in its forward-looking bookings, there are still a number of risks. Firstly, note that the company is currently operating at a significant net loss, which totaled $21.2 million in FY24. While there is a strong cash position, SEALSQ is not yet generating profits and the cash runway depends on its ability to convert pipeline into profitable revenue.

Then there is the commercialization risk, which the near-term potential rerating hinges a lot on. The post-quantum market is still emerging. There is no guarantee that SEALSQ's new technologies will be adopted quickly enough to justify its high R&D and acquisition costs. On top of that, revenue from its new quantum products is not expected until at least Q4 2025 or more likely in Q1 2026.

Takeaway

SEALSQ is a high-risk, high-reward quantum play that has moved beyond the "hype" stage. With its valuation now more rational, the company's success hinges on a handful of near-term catalysts - primarily, the timely commercialization of new post-quantum products and the successful revenue consolidation from acquisitions.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Enterprise Products Partners: Insiders Bought More In July, So Did I
data.description:
EPD stock: July insider activities in focus

I last covered Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (NYSE:EPD) on July 8 with an article entitled " Why Enterprise Products Partners Is A Stronger Dividend Stock Than ExxonMobil.” That article focused on EPD’s dividends and rated it as a Strong Buy. Since then, a new catalyst that caught my attention was its latest insider activities. These activities appear very unusual to me and triggered this full re-examination of the stock. In the end, I believe the insider purchases reflect a highly skewed reward/risk ratio in the stock and thus reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

More specifically, the next chart shows the insider transactions I was referring to. As seen, in July, there were two new purchases disclosure. These purchases totaled close to $1 million. The most recent purchase was disclosed on July 30, 2025, when 16,000 shares were purchased at a stock price of $31.55 by director Montgomery. A day earlier on July 29, 15,000 shares were purchased at a stock price of $31.34 by director Rutherford. Before these latest activities, the last batch of insider purchase was made Co-CEO Teague about 1 year ago in August 2024. These insider transactions piqued my interest for several reasons as elaborated on next.

EPD stock: why the insider activities stand out

First and foremost, the pattern of EPD’s insider transactions is very unusual in my eyes. There were no insider selling in reported in the past 2 years as you can teel from the screenshot above. This is in stark contrast to most of they other companies that I follow, where insider transactions were dominated by selling. The equity market as whole is also dominated by insider selling activities per the data provided below.

Secondly, the prices of the insiders’ purchases are also interesting to me. Note that the latest insider purchases were reported at a share price of ~$31 and the co-CEO’s purchases in 2024 were disclosure at a price of ~$29. These purchase prices all corresponded to a P/E ratio of around 11x with the EPS at the times when they were made. As to be explained later, I do believe an entry P/E of 11x provides a very favorable reward/risk profile under current conditions.

EPD stock: long-term growth prospects

Overall, I am optimistic about EPD long-term growth prospects, especially with the Trump administration’s emphasis on domestic energy production. EPD is well positioned for a crucial role here. As the largest member in this space, EPD has a presence in just about every major American shale play. Enterprise has also been investing large sums in capital projects in recent years, and I expect these investments to pay dividends – pun intended – for years to come. Over the past two years, the capital budget has averaged nearly $4.0 billion annually. For 2025, leadership estimates that funds used on growth projects ought to be in the neighborhood of $4.0 billion to $4.5 billion in 2025 before falling to $2.0 billion to $2.5 billion in 2026.

Assuming a growth investment of $2.25 billion (the mid-point of the guidance range), the reinvestment rate (RR) would be about 26% going forward even at this level. Such an RR, when combined with my projected ROCE (return on capital employed), could support a health growth sustainably. My method for projection long-term growth rate is detailed in this earlier article and here I will only quote the end results:

The method involves the return on capital employed (“ROCE”) and RR. The ROCE for EPD has been around 13.2% in recent years as seen in the chart below and I expect its ROCE to reach about 15% in the next few years as CAPEX spendings level off. With an estimated RR of 26%, its organic growth rate would be about 4% (15% ROCE x 26% RR = 3.9%).

Investors should not be under impressed by a growth rate of 4% at all for reasons to be explained next.

EPD stock: valuation and growth

Firstly, the growth rate projected by the above approach is the real growth rate. To obtain the notional growth rates we often quote, an escalation factor needs to be added and can push the notional growth rate higher.

Secondly, with the current P/E ratio for EPD, even a 4% growth rate could lead into robust total return potential in the longer term. As a reflection of its total return potential, let’s consider the PEGY ratio that Peter Lynch promoted for the following reason:

A revised version of the PEG ratio, the PEGY ratio is defined as the P/E ratio divided by the sum of the earnings growth rate and dividend yield. The idea behind the PEGY is very simple and effective (most effective ideas are simple). If a stock pays out a large part of its earnings as dividends, then investors do not need a high growth rate to enjoy healthy returns. And vice versa. And similar to the PEG ratio, his preference is a PEGY ratio of 1x or below.

In the case of EPD, its current FWD P/E ratio is 11.9x as seen in the chart below. Note that its current P/E ratio represents a discount relative to similar stocks. For instance, peers (like ET, KMI, and WMS) trades with an FWD ranging from about 13x to almost 27x. EPS provides a FWD dividend yield of 6.82% currently. Even with a 4% growth rate projection, the PEGY ratio worked out to be about 1.1x, quote close to the idea threshold of 1x.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks, both in the upside and downside directions are worth nothing. Energy stocks, especially pipe-line stocks, are debt-heavy. As such, stocks in this sector are not only sensitive to commodity price changes but also more sensitive to interest rates uncertainties than the overall economy. EPD is no exception. As an example, EPD's debt-to-equity ratio is more than 110%. As you can see from the table below, this is not unusual in the sector. Its close peers all feature similarly high leverage ratios. The debt-to-equity ratio is 134% for ET, 192% for WMB, and 102% for KMI.

As a compensation, Enterprise is well diversified both geographically and operationally. As aforementioned, EPD is involved in almost every shale play and does business in every segment of the midstream industry. Thanks to such diversification, the business is more resilient compared to its peers in more view and exhibited less cyclicality than almost all of its peers historically. As a reflection of such resilience, during the past 10 years, many companies in the industry were forced to cut dividends for various reasons (see the next chart below). But Enterprise not only didn’t cut its distribution, but also kept growing its payouts (for 27 consecutive years thus far).

To conclude, this follow-up article of EPD is mainly triggered by the disclosure of its insider activities in July 2025. Insiders’ purchases in July piqued my interest for several reasons and triggered a full reexamination of its growth, profitability, and valuations. My conclusion is that, at the entry P/E (around 11x) of these insider purchases, the stock indeed provides far more upside potential than downside risks.

data.title: WTI Crude Steadies Near As Traders Weigh Demand Slowdown Against Supply Risks
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

WTI crude oil (CL1:COM) is stabilizing after a bruising August that saw prices tumble from above $71 to near $62 before recovering into the $64 zone. On Friday, futures hovered at $64.30, extending a two-week rebound but still on course for the first monthly loss since the spring rally.

The technical shift from a clear downtrend to a rising recovery channel underscores a market in transition, where bearish fundamentals are being counterbalanced by supportive headlines.

WTI price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

On the four-hour chart, WTI now trades between $62 at the channel base and $66 at the ceiling. The cluster of moving averages around $64 reflects indecision, while the 200-period EMA near $64.68 remains the key tactical pivot. A sustained close above that level would be the first sign in weeks that bulls are regaining meaningful control. Momentum has improved, with RSI at 60 after climbing steadily from below 40 earlier this month, but conviction remains limited until resistance gives way.

Demand worries collide with supply risks

Fundamentals explain the hesitation. U.S. gasoline demand typically peaks at Labor Day, after which refineries lower runs for maintenance. The International Energy Agency recently forecast that global supply will outpace consumption in coming quarters, and the gradual return of OPEC+ production reinforces that view. These dynamics keep upside capped, with rallies meeting profit-taking as traders price in weaker autumn demand.

Yet the market has avoided a deeper breakdown. Rising geopolitical risks, particularly Ukrainian strikes on Russian export infrastructure, have added a risk premium, while talk of tighter sanctions enforcement by Washington continues to circulate. India’s appetite for Russian barrels also remains a wildcard, as purchases are expected to rise in September despite U.S. pressure. These forces have prevented bears from driving WTI decisively below $62.

Technical roadmap for September

Key levels remain well defined. Resistance sits at $65 and then $66 at the top of the rising channel. A daily close above $66, paired with stronger momentum, would likely open a run toward $68 and potentially $70, where the early August breakdown zone rests. Support is layered at $63.50 to $63.00, with the channel base at $62 serving as a critical test. A breakdown there would risk reopening $60.50 to $61, an area that previously attracted heavy buying.

Previously, analysis noted that August’s slide had left WTI vulnerable unless buyers could reclaim key moving averages. That observation remains relevant. While momentum has recovered and price action is constructive inside a rising channel, the market still requires a decisive breakout above $66 to shift sentiment firmly. Until then, oil remains range-bound, with traders leaning on technical edges while monitoring macro and geopolitical catalysts.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Clarus: DTC Worrying Bellwether For Consumer Response To Price Hikes
data.description:
Clarus Corporation (NASDAQ:CLAR) has some tariff exposure, probably around 6% of annualized operating expenses, and its price increases that we discussed in the previous coverage have only now been taken up by retailers after the close of the quarter. Negative mix effects from adventure are persisting from the inventory that they are trying to get rid of and they are about halfway done with clearing that. The DTC channel where the effects of the price increases are clearer, showed significant weakness. We would focus on this threat for the moment and see towards the end of the year when the profitability ends up being, also considering outstanding questions around tariffs.

Comments on results

There are a couple of things that concern us. The company was trying to eliminate lower-margin products and inventory at the beginning of the year. Some of that is still weighing on results, including soft demand from OEMs in adventure, though also emerging attempts to find new markets in underpenetrated geographies like the Americas. But now they are looking to reduce promotionality, and they also have to mitigate tariffs.

At Adventure, most of -- they source everything from China with a little bit coming from Australia -- made in region in Australia, okay? For Black Diamond, the majority of our inventory comes from Southeast Asia. I'm looking for the exact piece here: 25% from China, 31% from Taiwan, 15% from Vietnam, and 12% from the Philippines, with the remainder coming from elsewhere.

Michael J Yates, CFO of CLAR.

What concerns us is that the expectation for around $4 million in an annual tariff hit is based on assumptions that are now outdated. (At least there is an offset from layoffs generating $1 million in annual savings in the adventure segment as it's downsized).

However, looking ahead for the year, we expect the current tariffs, which include the 10% reciprocal tariffs on most countries, 50% on steel and aluminum and 55% on China to have a $3.4 million impact on earnings even after our mitigating measures. And of course, we are all waiting to see how the reciprocal tariff situation plays out. Should these tariffs land above the current 10% level, we expect to see an additional drag on earnings in 2025.

McNeil Seymour Fiske, Black Diamond President.

Tariffs are significantly ahead of the 10% mark for most of the trade deals at this point. Crudely, if the annual tariff impact was around 4% of annualized expenses before, with higher reciprocal tariffs it may be closer to 6% which is quite significant. More than expected pressure from tariffs is our first direct worry.

The price increases they are doing to offset these tariff impacts may really hit results. The business is primarily wholesale-based, and the price increases in the wholesale channel have not yet fully taken effect.

And while they didn't all take prices up immediately, that extra bit of time gives them time to plan, adjust and also confirm their fall 2025 orders. Whereas now, I think, with a lot of our peer group, they've taken prices up in July, and there's quite a bit of commotion now in the wholesale channel about how to adjust to those price changes right at the beginning of the season.

McNeil Seymour Fiske.

What worries us is the DTC softness where the planned price increases were quicker to implement because there are no middle steps like distributors or retailers.

From a strategy standpoint, we have moved D2C even more aggressively to a full price position. And we rolled out prices early -- new prices early in North America on D2C. And so the combination of less discounting and less promotion and higher prices, we saw a pretty sharp pullback in the D2C channel, 20% in North America, a little bit more modest in Europe.

McNeil Seymour Fiske.

We think there could be more downside for the wholesale sales as well. DTC is around 20% of the NA revenues in outdoor, which is the segment that is dominated by Black Diamond. All the tariff impacts are focused on this segment since Adventure has most of its markets outside the U.S. So far Black Diamond revenues are up 2% from the prior year, with NA up around 1.6% dragged down by the DTC component. Margins were up around 80 bps in Black Diamond to 34.9% and would have been higher if it hadn't been for FX hedging. Now with the disposal of the PIEPS business, still a source of legal issues, EBITDA is in positive territory on an adjusted basis for the outdoor segment.

Second quarter sales were $55.2 million compared to $56.5 million in the prior year's second quarter. The 2% decline in total sales was driven by the decrease in the Adventure segment of 8% and an increase in the Outdoor Segment of 1%. FX was a $0.5 million headwind, and the acquisition impact from RockyMounts was a $2.1 million tailwind.

Michael J Yates.

Even with inorganic help, the adventure segment was pressured by the weak demand for some legacy products and weak ticketing from lingering discounting activity. Rhino-Rack is not doing that well. MAXTRAX seems to be getting more traction and they've even managed to secure a German military contract and some new distributors in China.

Bottom Line

Macro is a concern for this business. The bottom line really is that we want to see how the shelves clear in a higher price situation. CLAR is working to defend and grow margin and profitability. While structural measures, like selling the unprofitable PIEPS business will help, we want to see how end consumers are going to respond to price increases taken up in July before we get excited about this stock at languishing prices. We do not want to take a position against a difficult economic direction and will focus on outstanding questions around the earnings direction for now, not thinking about valuations yet.

data.title: Tariffs Are Taxes
data.description:
The stated purpose of President Trump’s tariffs is to level the playing field by boosting US industries and punishing foreign exporters, but rather than hurting foreign exporters, the economic evidence shows American firms and consumers are hardest hit because tariffs are, in fact, taxes. The good news is that it’s a tax that will reduce the national debt. The bad news is that the US consumer may end up bearing more of the cost than expected, and this tax will increase inflation.

President Trump has initiated clever ways to address our incredible federal debt problem. The solutions to our mounting debt problem are to decrease spending and increase revenues, as follows:

  • Decreased spending: The stated savings from DOGE are estimated at $205 billion. These savings stem from increases in efficiency – a good thing, although the “real savings” are under debate.
  • Increased revenues: Tariffs increase revenues in much the same way as taxes, but tariffs trick us into supporting the tax because it “levels the (foreign exchange) playing field.” These revenues are estimated at $500 billion per year.
  • Combined improvement: Deficit spending could decrease by $700 billion from $2 trillion down to $1.3 trillion – a giant step in the right direction, ceteris paribus, but be aware that most of that reduction is paid by us as consumers.

The irony is that across the country, Americans Will Pay Less in Taxes Thanks to President Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill. The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA)makes the 2017 tax cuts permanent and introduces additional breaks, including deductions on tips and overtime pay, tax relief for seniors, and an expanded childcare tax credit. These tax cuts are dueling with tariff taxes.

Tariff mechanics

For a detailed description of how tariffs work and their economic effects, please see Oxford Economics’ Tariffs 101: What are they and how do they work?

Importers (U.S. entities) pay the tariff directly to U.S. Customs and Border Protection. Like other taxes, tariffs generate revenue for the government, which goes to the U.S. Treasury’s General Fund. The cost is generally passed on to consumers through higher prices. This is similar to other taxes, like sales taxes, where the end consumer bears the cost but doesn’t directly vote on each tax imposition.

The Tax Foundation lays out the details of the tariffs as a consumption tax in Who Pays Tariffs? Americans Will Bear the Costs of the Next Trade War. The study states that historically, "economists have found that foreign firms absorbed some burden of tariffs by lowering their prices, resulting in a combination of foreign businesses and domestic firms and consumers sharing the burden of tariffs. In contrast to past studies, however, recent studies have found the Trump tariffs were passed almost entirely through to US firms or final consumers."

This report is a compilation of several studies by economists who expect bad things to result from Trump’s tariffs.

Conclusion

No one likes to be fooled, but addressing our serious debt problem is good. Will this experiment work? Reducing the deficit should reduce inflation, but increasing prices will increase inflation. Which will prevail? IMHO, inflation will become a serious problem. If I’m right, investors should move to inflation-resistant approaches like TIPs, precious metals, real assets, etc.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: How Things Are Shaping Up
data.description:
The real U.S. economy continues to grow year-over-year, above two percent. And the compound rate of growth of the economy has been above 2.5 percent since the last recession that ended in March of 2020.

Inflation rose, as expected, in July. Concerns still exist about rising inflation in the coming months due to what is happening in the world of tariffs. But, as for now, the inflation rate remains around 2.5 percent, year-over-year.

And many analysts are expecting a round of "stagflation" to hit the U.S. economy soon.

But the stock market continues to set new historical highs.

Yesterday, both the Dow Jones Industrial Average and the Standard & Poor's 500 Stock Index closed at new records. The NASDAQ index closed just 8 points below its last historical high.

Furthermore, the Federal Reserve continues to pursue its policy of quantitative tightening, now almost three and one-half years old.

In the last banking week, ending August 27, 2025, the securities portfolio declined by $13.6 billion.

Since the current round of quantitative tightening began on September 18, 2024, the securities portfolio has been reduced by almost $400.0 billion, and the whole asset side of the Fed's balance sheet has been reduced by $506.1 billion.

Does the investment community like this performance?

I would say that the investment community likes this performance a whole lot!!!

Since the last few days of June 2025, the Standard & Poor's 500 index has hit 17 new historical highs. The NASDAQ index has hit 20 new historical highs. And the DJIA has hit 2 new historical highs.

One might have thought that President Trump would have been happy with this performance as a sign that the business community was continuing to buy into how the U.S. economy was evolving.

This, however, has not been the case as Trump has continued to criticize the Federal Reserve and its leadership and demanded that changes be made in the personnel at the Fed and the way the Fed conducts its business.

Market participants are now betting that the probability of the Federal Reserve dropping its policy rate of interest at the September meeting of the FOMC has risen. This has been the attitude in the market ever since Fed Chairman Jerome Powell spoke at the Fed's annual Jackson Hole conference.

However, Mr. Powell continues to talk about all the things that could still happen that would result in the Federal Reserve postponing any reduction in its rate.

Mr. Powell is just not ready to jump on the "rate reduction" bandwagon.

I am with him on this point.

There are still big uncertainties "out there" that could result in Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve changing course. Of course, the role-playing around the tariff situation is one of the biggest.

And several other issues could arise and cause the policy makers problems. The government deficit issue is still one that is cause for future concern, but for now, things still remain quiet.

Furthermore, as I have written elsewhere, the current situation in the foreign exchange markets points to a falling value for the U.S. dollar.

Since the first of January 2025, the value of the U.S. dollar has been falling.

The world has lost confidence in the U.S. dollar.

It is my view that the U.S. will have a hard time regaining the confidence of the world in the near future...the next couple of years.

This could cause the Federal Reserve some problems as it continues to try to maintain its current position of quantitative tightening.

So, there is much to be concerned about going forward in terms of what is happening to the Federal Reserve and to the conduct of monetary policy.

Other analysts have pointed to the growth of the money stock over the past year. Here is a picture of the performance of the M2 money stock over the past 52 weeks.

The year-over-year rate of growth of the M2 money stock is 4.8 percent.

I think that the money stock numbers give us confidence that even though the Federal Reserve has been reducing the size of its securities portfolio over the past three and one-half years, there are still sufficient reserves in the banking system to support the continued growth of the U.S. economy.

Given this situation, I believe that the Federal Reserve can continue along, much as Mr. Powell and the FOMC have been doing, and the economy will find that it has sufficient amounts of "money" to support the current growth rate of the economy.

That is, the investment community appears to have a lot of "trust" in the Federal Reserve System and what it is doing.

The rising level of the stock market is an indication of that.

This support allows the U.S. economy to continue to grow at a 2.5 percent annual rate over the next several years.

The current Federal Reserve position allows the price level to grow in the 2.0 percent to 2.5 percent range over this time period.

Still, there are a lot of uncertainties to be resolved. They must be responded to with the full attention of the policymakers.

data.title: Solid Power: My Favorite Advanced Battery Stock, With The Potential To End Gas Cars
data.description:
Over recent years, we've seen the price premium for electric vehicles over gas decline as the industry scales production. Electric car sales were very high last year, but have stagnated and potentially declined. This tracks the general trend in total vehicle sales, potentially falling due to tariff impacts and economic strain. The coming end to EV tax credits may exacerbate this decline:

The top reasons, beyond cost, that most people do not buy electric vehicles are lower range and longer charging times. However, as I've discussed regarding Toyota (TM), solid-state battery technology promises to solve these two issues. These batteries may have more energy capacity in a smaller space. According to Toyota, its first solid-state battery vehicles will begin production in 2027, charging up to 80% in 10 minutes with a 620-mile range, also with a lower risk of fires. These cars should have 50-80% more range, in theory, than current lithium-ion batteries.

Even if we assume advanced batteries may be among the most important innovations this decade (which I suspect), we cannot assume all "solid-state battery" companies are worth buying. As I detailed regarding NANO Nuclear (NNE), a firm with a great idea may not be a good investment if its valuation is too high, its cash burn is too extreme, or there is no clear path to profitability.

One of the most popular solid-state battery stocks is Solid Power (NASDAQ:SLDP). The firm explains its product as:

Solid Power’s all-solid-state batteries remove the flammable liquid electrolyte and polymer separator layer in a traditional lithium-ion battery and replace it with a solid layer. This thin, solid layer acts as a barrier to keep the anode and cathode from touching one another, which would short the battery. It also acts as a conductive electrolyte

...Solid Power produces the most advanced known solid electrolytes. Only Solid Power develops and produces sulfide-based solid electrolytes at pilot scale and tests in large format cells produced on a scalable production line

...Solid Power’s core sulfide-based solid electrolyte technology uses earth-abundant materials. We expect to scale electrolyte production to power 800,000 electrified vehicles using our all-solid-state battery cells annually by 2028.

As investors, we must translate its technological claims into profitability projections and gauge that against its valuation and capital needs. A skeptical take may be wise given its short interest is higher at 9.9% while the stock fell around 7% Friday morning. It's up around 195% over the past year, possibly creating a high risk of short-term reversal.

Is Solid Power's Hype or a Paradigm Shift?

There is an immense amount of investor focus on emerging innovation today. Although we are seeing this technological change, particularly in power tech, I am wary of companies that may overpromise. From its last investor presentation, we can see that it has only 230 employees despite a market capitalization of nearly $1B. It has over 20 patents and far more in pending applications.

It is working to design, manufacture, and sell electrolyte batteries to "Tier 1 battery manufacturers and automotive OEMs, aiming to work with and not compete with these firms." The "sulfide-based solid electrolyte" battery is claimed to be "CAPEX-light," based on a powder that can be sold to "companies pursuing their own sulfide-based all-solid-state batteries." They're also creating "energy-dense pouch cells," used for a similar purpose. The company is collaborating with BMW, testing an i7 with Solid Power cells. In August, the company also announced that it had completed factory acceptance in South Korea, aiming for 75 mt capacity.

Solid Power generates some revenue as a result of selling test cells, though a very small amount. Its TTM revenue is ~$22.7B with some growth, but its price-to-sales ratio is very high at 34X:

The recent increase in its valuation should be an indication that its growth outlook is improving. There is only one Wall Street analyst making projections on its sales and income, seeing sales rise at a rate of around 20-100% per year through 2032 to ~$800M. The same analyst places its 2032 EPS at $0.88, giving it a potential long-term forward "P/E" of 5.2X, albeit seven years into the future.

Its price today makes sense compared to that target, but the execution risk is significant. Its 2024 DOE award notes that "Solid Power plans to significantly increase its annual production capacity, first to 75 metric tons in 2026 and then to 140 metric tons in 2028, in order to meet anticipated demand." The company is on a ramp to quickly scale up, and if it can do so in a "capital light" manner, as it says, it could reach these lofty long-term targets.

From its presentation, it claims that 50% of the solid-state battery makers are looking to the Sulifide SSB technology it produces for automotive batteries. That implies there should be a good market for this product. The electric vehicle battery market is forecasted to grow to $252B by 2032 (around $85-$100B today). So, if we're projecting $800M in revenue by 2032, I think that is realistic given the growing size of the EV battery market and the apparent demand for the tech.

I'm not a materials scientist or expert in battery technology, but I can see how the promises of solid-state batteries should accelerate EV adoption and sales over the next decade. With that in mind and given Solid Power's purported IP and capital-light edge, I think these projections are realistic on paper. It's that crucial stage of executing scaled manufacturing that I think will make or break Solid Power. The key factors I want to see are that the development phase is on time and on budget, limiting the risk of excessive dilutions or falling behind the competition.

Its cash position is decent compared to its cash burn rate. Its liquidity was about $231M last quarter, offset by TTM operating cash flow of $64. Most of its cash-flow metrics are stable, though its TTM CapEx is also low:

So again, Solid Power is transitioning from a semi-proven concept to a manufacturing company with scale. Its CapEx is lower TTM, meaning we may see more draw on cash over the next two years. Combined with its $64M operating cash flow draw, I think Solid Power will likely need to raise capital, which may dilute equity. Luckily, its price-to-sales ratio is high enough that its cost of capital is pretty low.

Early revenue companies are challenging to value. However, growth potential should correlate with employees who drive that growth. Compared to peers Amprius (AMPX), SES AI Corp. (SES), and QuantumScape (QS), a basket of "Advanced Battery Stocks" based on Deep Value Investing's research, we can see Solid Power's market cap is lower per employee:

In my view, a lower market-cap per employee ratio may be an indication of undervaluation. If we combine that with the "capital-light" growth model, its decent cash management, the massive potential total addressable market for SSBs, and the preliminary data (mostly based on Solid Power's material) indicating its IP may be best for automotive applications, I think SLDP may be a decent (highly speculative) growth stock.

The Bottom Line

I am speculatively bullish on SLDP. I wouldn't bet the ranch on it, probably just 1-2% of my portfolio. The fact is that the amount of uncertainty it faces is huge. That includes execution risk, dilution risk, a technology that is unproven at commercial scale, and seemingly lower demand for EVs. Based simply on the first chart showing weaker EV and overall car sales, I am cautious that economic strain may cause hype to fade from the EV market over the next year, particularly without tax credits.

Still, I am very bullish on solid-state battery technology. In my view, the possibility of doubling EV range and cutting charging times would make it a no-brainer over gas-powered cars. Among the advanced battery companies, SLDP is my favorite due to its potentially lower valuation and the fact that it is generating revenue through testing deals with car companies. It is proving commercial viability; the question is whether it can scale that and maintain and grow its competitive edge. Still, an $828M company compared to what may be a $250B+ EV battery market (and perhaps more from non-EV needs), I see a lot of potential upside.

Based on the trend in its stock, which mirrors the trend in weaker EV sales, I think it may be due for a larger correction. In fact, I think it's most likely that SDLP will fall over the coming weeks and months. In my view, that would be an accumulation opportunity for a small position, as long as nothing changes in its fundamentals. So I do not think its short-term outlook is good, but I am sufficiently bullish long-term that my overall view is "slightly bullish," with the intent of building a larger position on a pullback.

data.title: Markets Brace For September Showdown: Fed Meeting Looms As Small Caps Lead
data.description:
Stocks stumbled on the final trading day of August, but the S&P 500 ETF (SP500, SPY) posted a 2% monthly rally. The Nasdaq 100 ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ) rose 1%, with a mixed bag of returns among the Magnificent Seven stocks. The real August star was the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:IWM). The market’s “little guys” soared 7%. International equities, as measured by the Vanguard FTSE All-World Ex-US ETF (VEU) logged a 4% climb, posting impressive alpha to the domestic stock market.

In the bond market, the yield on the benchmark 10-year Treasury note (US10Y) dipped slightly, resulting in a respectable 1% total return in the iShares Aggregate Bond ETF (AGG). There was action under the surface, though, as the 30-year Treasury bond rate rose, while the yield on the 2-year Treasury note fell. The market anticipates that the Fed will cut interest rates next month.

Elsewhere, gold shined. The SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) added 5% to settle at an all-time high on the weekly chart. Shares of gold miners did even better—the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) catapulted 22% to finish at an all-time high on a total return basis. Supporting gold was a weaker U.S. dollar (down 2%). In crypto, the iShares Bitcoin ETF (IBIT) fell 7%, but the iShares Ethereum ETF (ETHA) jumped 15%.

Small Caps Stood Out in August, S&P 500 Posts A Modest Gain

Ether and Gold Miners Skyrocketed in August

Digging into the sectors, two surprising niches rose to the top ranks. Materials (XLB) and Health Care (XLV) tallied 5% returns. Consumer Discretionary (XLY) held the August lead until the final day of the month, when a bout of risk-off pressure swept across the markets. Bringing up the rear was Utilities (XLU). Joining the “utes” in the red were Industrials (XLI) and Information Technology (XLK).

Savvy investors may pick up on a theme. The “AI” megatrend lagged, as value and real assets did well.

Materials and Healthcare Led in August, AI Plays Sank

Turning to the economy, all eyes are on the jobs market. Next Friday, we’ll get the August nonfarm payrolls report. Economists expect a tepid sub-100,000 employment gain, with the unemployment rate holding near year-to-date highs of 4.3%.

Looking back, the July labor market report was much weaker than expected. Major downward revisions to the May and June jobs data pointed to a clear slowdown in the employment situation. That may have been the deciding factor in the Fed potentially cutting interest rates come September 17. The three-month average pace of monthly job growth is now just 35,000.

A Weak July Jobs Report May Prompt Fed Easing Sooner Rather Than Later

It’s not all doom and gloom, however. July’s Retail Sales report, put out by the U.S. Census Bureau, rebounded to 3.9% on a year-on-year basis. That was comfortably above the prevailing inflation rate. Still, critics contend that consumers are simply having to pay more for many goods impacted by President Trump’s tariffs.

We’ll know more about the extent to which high import duties are hitting sticker prices on the store shelves in the weeks to come. On August 7, new, higher reciprocal tariffs went into effect. Businesses are footing most of the bill so far, but consumers are anticipated to absorb more of the levies’ hit as we approach the end of the year.

U.S. Retail Sales Bounced Back in July

Speaking of inflation, we got the July PCE Price Index data on the final trading day of August.

The in-line report didn’t move markets much, but it confirmed that the underlying pace of consumer price increases tracks materially above the Fed’s 2% YoY headline PCE target.

PCE Inflation Running Above the Fed's Target

Market pundits, including yours truly, are already looking ahead to 2026.

Considering that the Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow tool shows a solid 3.5% Q3 real GDP growth rate, the odds of a technical recession (two consecutive quarters of negative GDP growth) occurring this year are low—just 8% according to online prediction markets.

Low Chance of a U.S. Recession in 2025

As for the Fed, the Committee is likely to resume its rate-cutting cycle when it votes next, on September 17. It remains to be seen if Stephen Miran, the temporary replacement for the former FOMC voting member Adriana Kugler, will cast a ballot.

The Senate is expected to take up his confirmation when Congress returns from recess after Labor Day.

High Odds of a September Fed Rate Cut

The August stock market rally came during and after what was a very strong Q2 earnings season. The S&P 500’s EPS growth rate is likely to tally near 12%--another in a string of double-digit YoY bottom-line growth gains. A more measured 7.5% EPS growth rate is projected for Q3.

The bulls can point to CY 2025 and CY 2026 per-share earnings forecasts that are solidly on the rise.

S&P 500 Now Trades > 22x Earnings

The bears counter by pointing out that September seasonality is downright bearish. Since 2010, the S&P 500 has featured an average return of -0.8%, the worst of the 12 months.

Calendar trends have not played out like they usually do, though, as the SPX rose nicely in August (another historically weak month).

S&P 500 ETF: Weak September Trends

The Bottom Line

Amid all the chaos brewed by the Trump Administration toward the Federal Reserve and inflation worries amid higher tariffs, stocks rose in August. Small caps, seen as the most sensitive to rising prices economywide, soared.

September is poised to be a showdown between the bulls and the bears, with a key Fed interest rate decision right in the middle of the often spooky month.

data.title: Assessing Franklin BSP Realty's Performance For Q2
data.description:
By Scott Kennedy, Produced with Colorado Wealth Management Fund

Introduction

Franklin BSP Realty Trust's (NYSE:FBRT) results this quarter were mostly as expected. Their earnings/EAD dropped more than expected. The company’s big purchase of NewPoint could help earnings recover, but it will take time. At recent prices, we view FBRT as a buy, but cautious investors may want to wait.

Commentary

  • Quarterly BV Fluctuation: Very Minor – Minor Outperformance (1.1% Variance).
  • Adjusted Core Earnings/EAD: Modest - Notable Underperformance ($0.058 Variance).

A largely “as expected” quarter regarding Franklin BSP Realty Trust's BV in my opinion. If anything a very minor – minor outperformance. FBRT recorded a very minor quarterly BV decrease whereas I projected a minor decrease. Partially to a very minor net realized loss/write-off/REO transfer of 1 previously reserved-for property, FBRT recorded a quarterly net decrease to the company’s CECL reserves of $4 million (decreases are generally good). Part of this reserve decrease was also due to FBRT’s smaller investment portfolio size. Still, in my opinion, this was a bit “overly optimistic” due to the fact FBRT added 3 new loans to the company’s “watch list” during Q2 2025. There was basically no accompanying new CECL reserves added for this event. In comparison, correctly assuming a particular medium-sized multifamily property would be transferred to REO assets and a couple more loans would be added to the watch list, I projected a quarterly net CECL reserves increase of ($15) million. As noted above, FBRT continued to actively take possession of an underlying troubled loan/property and transferred/converted it into an REO asset. REO assets are tested for impairment as opposed to specifically having CECL reserves (which pertains to loans). FBRT’s lower CECL reserves when compared to my expectations directly led to a BV outperformance of $0.21 per common share which was the vast majority of the company’s overall quarterly BV outperformance of $0.17 per common share. The remainder of FBRT’s quarterly BV variance was basically the ($0.058) per common share underperformance within the company’s adjusted core earnings/EAD (discussed next).

FBRT’s modest-notable adjusted core earnings/EAD underperformance, which reverses applicable realized gains (losses) incurred during the quarter (similar sub-sector methodology), was mainly due to the following: 1) (6.5%) decrease in investment portfolio size when including REO sales (my projection was a mean decrease of only (2%)); 2) slightly lower investment portfolio effective coupon (7.85% during Q2 2025 versus my projection of 7.90%); and 3) slight underperformance in total operational expenses (mainly other expenses). Regarding origination volume, FBRT funded $321 and only $91 million in loans during Q1 2025 and Q2 2025, respectively. Simply put, a modest-notable quarterly decrease which underperformed my mean projection of $225 million. In addition, FBRT had loan prepayments/repayments/amortization of ($353) and ($317) million during Q1 2025 and Q2 2025, respectively. Simply put, still fairly elevated. In comparison, I projected quarterly loan prepayments/repayments/amortization of ($275) million during Q2 2025. In addition, FBRT sold ($84) million of the company’s accruing commercial MBS bonds during Q2 2025. This also directly resulted in lower net spread income/adjusted core earnings/EAD which was disappointing.

So, FBRT’s minor quarterly BV decline largely matched expectations. However, FBRT’s notable quarterly adjusted core earnings/EAD decline was a negative surprise. While I correctly assumed “some” slowdown in operations in anticipation of the NewPoint Holdings JV LLC (NewPoint) acquisition (hence my projected minor quarterly adjusted core earnings/EAD decrease), the actual severity of this metric’s decline was disappointing.

While I continue to believe FBRT’s adjusted core earnings/EAD will increase as a direct result of the NewPoint acquisition, I also believe it is now a much harder “task” to increase the company’s adjusted core earnings/EAD from $0.227 per common share back to the current dividend level of $0.355 per common share. I know management believes they can move adjusted core earnings/EAD back to this level but I believe that is if “all the stars align” per se. However, that does not mean I believe a dividend cut is a 100% probability at this time. Management appears to be pretty confident adjusted core earnings/EAD will move back to the dividend within the next year or so. Still, we have seen that “tune played before” regarding another fairly similar mREIT peer not too long ago and we know how that played out (Ready Capital (RC)).

If it was not for the NewPoint acquisition, I probably would have downgraded FBRT’s recommendation ranges by (5%) or even (7.5%). However, I always try to remain non-bias in my assessments/judgements.

As such, along with my credit risk analysis below, I/we are performing a (2.5%) recommendation range downgrade to FBRT. However, this does not result in a risk/performance rating downgrade (remains at a 4.5). FBRT continues to have commercial real estate/multifamily loan exposure. Credit risk is something I am closely watching with FBRT. Potential credit risk had another “uptick” during Q2 2025 (2nd quarter in a row; discussed below). As such, I continue to anticipate new non-accrual loans at some point during 2025 – 2026 (at the very least additional quick transfers to REO assets and/or write-offs on loan/property sales).

Change or Maintain

  • BV/NAV Adjustment (BV/NAV Used Interchangeably): Our projection for current BV/NAV per share was adjusted: Up $0.15 (To Account for the Actual 6/30/2025 BV/NAV Vs. Prior Projection). Price targets have already been adjusted to reflect the change in BV/NAV. The update is included in the card below and the subscriber spreadsheets.
  • Percentage Recommendation Range (Relative to CURRENT BV/NAV): (2.5%) Downgrade Results in a ($0.35) Per Share Recommendation Range/Price Target Decrease.
  • Risk/Performance Rating: No Change. Remains at 4.5.

Earnings Results

Note: BV at the end of the quarter. Subscriber spreadsheets and targets use current estimates, not trailing values.

Valuation

Ending Notes/Commentary

As a reminder, regarding setting an appropriate CECL, a very high degree of managerial judgement occurs/specialized expertise is needed. All of FBRT’s commercial whole loans are level 3 assets per ASC 820. Simply put, there is not a widespread, active marketplace for commercial whole loan pricing/valuations as each mortgage (and underlying collateral) is unique. This makes setting valuations difficult; especially pertaining to impairment testing.

Regarding credit metrics, similar to the prior quarter, there was elevated activity that occurred during Q2 2025 regarding resolutions, transfers to REO, and/or new watch list loans. Simply looking at “top-line” metrics really does not tell the story. Upon performing a “deep dive” analysis, FBRT added 3 loans to the company’s “watch list” as of 6/30/2025 when compared to 3/31/2025. This was an AZ office, TX multifamily, and NC multifamily loan. I correctly projected both multifamily loans would be added to FBRT’s watch list during Q2 2025. However, I was surprised the AZ office loan was (a bit cautionary/disappointing). However, within these 3 loans, 0 loans were placed on non-accrual status as of 6/30/2025 which is “cautiously optimistic” so far. In addition, as noted above, a previous watch list TX multifamily non-accrual loan was transferred to a REO asset and was partially written-off at the time of transfer.

Regarding FBRT’s acquisition of NewPoint, this officially closed on 7/1/2025. As such, the financial impacts of this acquisition will begin during Q3 2025. Management remains pleased regarding this commercial R/E finance company acquisition (who mainly specializes in multifamily operations). To reiterate, I believe this proposed acquisition is a net positive for FBRT (hence only the (2.5%) recommendation range downgrade on poor adjusted core earnings/EAD performance during Q2 2025 instead of a larger downgrade if this acquisition did not occur). NewPoint’s financials were just provided yesterday. As such, “final touches” to FBRT’s earnings modeling will occur in the near future (once earnings season winds down in a few weeks). I am not anticipating notable changes to my modeling though (which already was incorporating NewPoint’s projected performance).

Even with a minor recommendation range downgrade, FBRT is currently deemed to be modestly undervalued. However, as echoed over the last 7 quarters, cautious subscribers may want to wait until more market participants believe it is safer to invest in commercial real estate as a whole (yes, even multifamily loans). I currently believe, in a couple years, FBRT’s stock price should be higher than its current per share amount. However, it will likely be a “bumpy/see-sawing” road over the next couple quarters (as it recently has been).

data.title: Why I'm Avoiding Nuvation Bio Despite Its Recent FDA Approval
data.description:
Overview

This is my first look at Nuvation Bio (NYSE:NUVB), which makes this a good time to show my readers how I go about researching biotechnology stocks. So, Nuvation markets IBTROZI (taletrectinib), a TKI for ROS-1-positive NSCLC. Taletrectinib was originally developed by Daiichi-Sankyo and was licensed (then in Phase 1 development) to AnHeart Therapeutics in 2018. In 2024, Nuvation acquired AnHeart Therapeutics (then in Phase 2 studies). Following positive Phase 2 data, IBTROZI achieved FDA approval in June 2025, joining a few other ROS1+ TKIs:

In the U.S., it is estimated that there will be 226,650 new cases of lung cancer in 2025. ~87% of cases are NSCLC (~197k NSCLC/year). It is estimated that 1%-2% of NSCLC (1.5% at the midpoint) are ROS1+ (~2,958 new ROS1+ NSCLC/year).

IBTROZI was priced around $29,500 per month (WAC), which was in line with other ROS1+ TKIs (e.g., AUGTYRO costs ~$29,000/month). Just days following its approval, the NCCN updated its guidelines to list IBTROZI as a preferred agent in both first-line and subsequent therapy for ROS1+ NSCLC. It is preferred due to its relatively good tolerability and activity against brain metastases. I know firsthand that oncologists reference NCCN guidelines when making treatment decisions, so having “preferred” status goes a long way in adoption. With that said, Bristol Myers Squibb’s (BMY) AUGTYRO is a reasonable alternative per guidelines I frequently follow, such as UpToDate. Both ROZLYTREK and XALKORI are “less preferred.”

According to Verified Market Reports, targeted drug ROS1 inhibitors for NSCLC are a $1.2 billion market that is forecasted to increase to $3.5 billion by 2033. The math adds up:

IBTROZI $29,500/month → $354,000/year → x 2,958 new ROS1+ NSCLC = $1.047 billion.

Notably, no ROS1+ TKI has come close to this. The estimate above is a naive TAM. WAC seldom = net price due to gross-to-net discounts, dose holds, adherence, etc. Moreover, not every ROS1+ patient receives a ROS1-TKI immediately. Some patients are too frail. Some patients start chemo-IO before genotype returns. Some die before treatment.

AUGTYRO is a good use case. Bristol Myers Squibb gained this asset from its $4.1 billion acquisition of Turning Point Therapeutics in 2022. However, after it was approved in 2023, the drug generated just $38 million worldwide in 2024, prompting the pharma giant to record a $1.4 billion impairment charge. Despite IBTROZI’s “preferred” status, AUGTYRO, so long as it remains on the market, will serve as a worthy competitor. Its head start could lock up a portion of the market for years. As another Seeking Alpha analyst has pointed out, Nuvation will have to conduct post-marketing studies on IBTROZI. Moreover, its key patents/orphan drug exclusivity may limit its market exclusivity to just 2033.

In this light, the base case for U.S. ROS1 opportunity per brand is much closer to the low hundreds of millions at maturity. Certainly, no more than $300-$400 million per year.

Financial Health

In its second-quarter earnings report, Nuvation generated $1.2 million in net product revenue from the initial U.S. sales of IBTROZI following its June launch. Reportedly, 70 patients had started treatment by July 31. It also made $3.6 million in revenue from its collaboration with Innovent for taletrectinib in China. R&D and SG&A expenses were $27.4 million and $38.5 million, respectively. Its net loss in Q2 was $59 million.

Nuvation ended Q2 with $607.7 million in cash and short-term investments. Its “cash burn” (cash from operations) was $48.2 million in Q2. If we divide its “cash burn” by its cash and investments, it implies a “cash runway” of approximately three years. However, this is just an estimate based on historical data. Notably, Nuvation’s cash from operations has steadily increased in recent quarters ahead of IBTROZI’s commercialization.

Pipeline

Meanwhile, Nuvation is advancing safusidenib (mIDH1) in diffuse IDH1-mutant glioma (Phase 2). This drug has demonstrated early clinical activity in glioma. However, it faces some challenges. Namely, it will need clear advantages (i.e., tolerability, QoL, sequencing benefit) to trump other IDH inhibitors with glioma activity.

VORANIGO (vorasidenib), a dual IDH1/2, was FDA-approved for grade 2 IDH-mutant glioma last year. Being an IDH1-only inhibitor, safusidenib won’t cover IDH2-mutant tumors. Others, like ivosidenib and olutasidenib, are also being explored for gliomas. The IDH1 glioma market opportunity is roughly comparable to ROS1+ NSCLC.

The Bottom Line

To wrap up, after carefully reviewing 1) IBTROZI data, 2) treatment recommendations, 3) market data, and 4) past and current performance from ROS1+ TKIs, as well as incorporating my own clinical experience, I've concluded that, in light of its small opportunities in the competitive ROS1+ NSCLC and IDH1 glioma markets, Nuvation appears optimistically valued ($450 EV). Bear in mind that $250 million, for example, in "peak annual revenue" for IBTROZI won't come out to $250 million in "free cash flow" (minus COGS, SG&A expenses, etc.). Moreover, "free cash flow" years from now is heavily discounted. I suspect that NUVB's high short interest (20%) may be in the anticipation of a challenging and costly launch for IBTROZI. While the favorable NCCN guidelines assist in de-risking IBTROZI, competition remains relevant, and the opportunity, in reality, is pretty small.

Likewise, Nuvation’s development of safusidenib follows a similar pathway to IBTROZI. In IDH1 glioma, we have a similarly sized market. Moreover, safusidenib may come off as a “me-too” drug if it doesn’t clearly differentiate itself. To be honest, I just don’t see the appeal in NUVB and feel the risk/reward profile skews negative, meriting a “Sell” rating.

Risks to my Sell rating include rapid adoption of IBTROZI that surprises to the upside, positive clinical data for safusidenib, and promising developments in Nuvation’s early-stage assets.
